title: Roast rack of lamb
subtitle: French-trimmed rack with a herb and mustard crust
excerpt: A French-trimmed rack of lamb coated in Dijon mustard and a breadcrumb and herb crust, then roasted until pink and served with a simple jus. The most elegant of the British lamb roasts.
sourceNotes: Rack of lamb with a herb crust is a classic of French and British restaurant cooking, documented in British cookbooks from the mid-twentieth century. The mustard-and-herb-crumb crust technique is standard in twentieth-century British and French cookery. Richard Olney and Jane Grigson both document the dish in their respective works.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The crust can be applied and the rack refrigerated for up to 4 hours before roasting.

content.text: A rack of lamb is one of the most impressive pieces of meat you can roast, and one of the fastest: it needs only 20 to 25 minutes at high heat. The herb crust adheres to the fat side and protects the meat during roasting while forming a golden, fragrant crust. The mustard acts as the adhesive; without it the crumb tends to fall off.

Ask your butcher for a French-trimmed rack: the bones are cleaned of meat and fat for presentation. Two racks (one per person if generous, or shared between two with sides) is the right quantity for a dinner for two. Rest the meat for 10 minutes before carving between the bones into individual cutlets.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Mix {{breadcrumbs-panko}} of panko breadcrumbs with the chopped parsley, chopped rosemary, crushed garlic, and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Season well. Season {{rack-of-lamb}} racks of lamb all over with salt and pepper. Heat an ovenproof frying pan or roasting tin over high heat. Sear the racks fat-side down for 3 minutes until the fat has coloured, then on the meaty sides briefly.

Place fat-side up. Brush {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard generously over the fat cap of each rack. Press the herb crumb mixture firmly onto the mustard — it should stick in a thick, even layer. Roast in the oven for 18 to 22 minutes for pink lamb: the internal temperature should read 57°C at the thickest point. Rest on a board for 10 minutes, then carve between the bones into individual cutlets.

items.symptom: Crust has fallen off during cooking
items.cause: Not enough mustard as adhesive, or crumb not pressed on firmly enough
items.fix: Apply the mustard in a generous layer and press the crumb on firmly with your palm. Allow the crusted racks to sit for 5 minutes before roasting so the crumb can adhere.

items.symptom: Lamb is overcooked and grey inside
items.cause: Cooked for too long, or oven temperature was too low
items.fix: Use a thermometer: 57°C for pink, 65°C for medium. The racks cook faster than expected at 200°C fan. Start checking at 18 minutes.

items.symptom: Crust is burned but meat is undercooked
items.cause: Oven is hotter than stated, or rack was too close to the top element
items.fix: Move the rack to a lower shelf and cover the crust loosely with foil if it is browning too fast.

items.symptom: Fat cap is pale and not rendered
items.fix: Sear fat-side down for a full 3 minutes over high heat before roasting. Rendered fat is much better to eat than soft, unrendered fat.

content.text: Rack of lamb with a herb crust is one of those dishes that sits at the intersection of French technique and British ingredient: lamb has been the defining meat of British pastoral cooking for centuries, and the French-trimmed rack presentation and crumb-crusting technique came from the classic restaurant tradition. In British domestic cooking it became associated with special occasions and dinner parties from the 1970s onwards, when French techniques filtered into home cooking through books like Elizabeth David's and later Delia Smith's. It remains one of the most reliable ways to impress at a dinner table while requiring relatively little effort.
